description.value:
Unique sophisticated analog calculator/orrery of gold-plated bronze, palm-sized, with many wheels, gears, and interfacing dials. Offers 32 dial-based changing displays on front. Stern warning in Greek, Sumerian, and Latin on plain back warning never to open it on pain of death (those who ignore disappear from existence when next alone). Has dozens of intermeshing gears tracking sun/moon through zodiac, predicting eclipses, calculating sunrise/sunset from star positions, and precisely tracking certain stars and major planets. Anyone with 30%+ in Navigation, Science (Astronomy), or Science (Augury) gains +1 bonus die. Rumored Sumerian origin, passed through hands of Pythagoras and other great scientists before reaching eccentric Roman collector Titus Labienus Luscinus. Warning also inscribed not to use under full moonlight. Under full moon, new display appears on back (0/1 SAN loss) with two additional dials and button running 0-10,000 (Sumerian base-60 cuneiform), giving 100 million combinations. Pushing button causes display to fade and user feels pulled in certain direction (1 SAN/day to resist). Pull aids safe travel to destination (coordinates north and east around globe with Babylon at 0,0; farthest point 5000,5000). Starfinder protects user from damage/death via Luck roll (harm befalls someone else nearby instead), but sevenfold: avoided sword thrust causes seven deaths. Using once realizing this costs 1D6 SAN per use.

TypeMagical Calculator/Orrery
Used ByAnyone
EffectTracks celestial movements; predicts eclipses; calculates astronomical positions; navigation bonus; can pull user to keyed destination but at terrible cost; can be dangerous under full moonlight
description.keeper: Effect: Tracks celestial movements; predicts eclipses; calculates astronomical positions; navigation bonus; can pull user to keyed destination but at terrible cost; can be dangerous under full moonlight
